paragraphs.summary: This Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) supervisory statement is relevant to firms in scope of Solvency II, including the Society of Lloyd's (collectively 'insurers' or ‘firms’ throughout the SS),

By clearly and consistently explaining its expectations of insurers and audit firms in relation to the particular areas addressed, the PRA seeks to advance its statutory objectives of ensuring the saf

sections.chapter_title: Governing body’s responsibility for the SFCR

paragraphs.summary: The Reporting Part of the PRA Rulebook requires that an insurer must have in place a written policy ensuring the ongoing appropriateness of any information disclosed and that t
As well as having a written policy in place to ensure the ongoing appropriateness of any information disclosed, the PRA expects that the governing body should be satisfied that:

The PRA expects the governing body to acknowledge and evidence in writing its responsibility for the SFCR and make this available to potential readers of the SFCR by signing the SFCR and attaching the

External audit of the SFCR refers to an independent auditor gathering sufficient appropriate evidence to provide an overall reasonable assurance opinion that the public disclosure in scope complies in

PRA rules apply whenever a SFCR is disclosed, whether at the solo or, where relevant, at the group and sub group level. Information in relation to undertakings included in the SFCR (including undertak

Where information for the group SFCR is compiled in accordance with sectoral rules (as required under Solvency II), the group auditor should undertake an assessment of whether that information has bee

The auditor is not expected to express an opinion on the validity of an approval, waiver or other supervisory determination. Instead approvals, waivers and supervisory determinations provided by the c
External Audit 4.1(3) requires that the auditor read and consider all information disclosed by the firm in its SFCR that is not subject to

The PRA notes that standards and guidance for auditors are issued by the Financial Reporting Council (FRC) which apply to non-statutory audit engagements of the type required by the PRA’s rules. The P
Auditing standards and guidance will be updated by the FRC from time to time. The PRA may update this supervisory statement accordingly as appropriate.

The scale of the matching adjustment (ie the extent to which the MA impacts on technical provisions) is within scope of audit where the PRA’s external audit rules apply.6 This reflects the fact that t

However, the interaction of the SFCR external audit rules and those relating to firms’ use of the matching adjustment (MA) may be complex. This reflects the nature of the MA, the role of the PRA in su
In the context of the External Audit Part, the main requirements relating to the MA can usefully be separated between the related but distinct:
The PRA takes into account the firm’s description of its process to calculate the MA during the MA application process. It does not approve the firm’s calculation methodology as part of that process.
In forming the opinion required by Rules 2.1 and [4.1 of the External Audit Part](/pra-rules/external-audit#1e4ddc8e324d4dc9a7eb0d8d5e7ae9

However, to provide the audit opinion required in relation to the SFCR, to the extent it is material to their opinion, auditors are expected to consider the scale of the MA claimed by the firm. This r
The PRA is not prescriptive about the audit work necessary to support the auditor's opinion on the SFCR, or the approach that it should take in forming its view. The PRA notes that the audit approach

The MA calculation does not form part of the eligibility conditions under which the PRA would be obliged (if the conditions were met) to grant permission in accordance with regulation 4(2) of the IRPR

Where the PRA has carried out work to review the MA calculation (including the methodologies, processes, or judgements that contribute to the MA), an auditor of the SFCR would still be expected to inc

If through the course of their work, an auditor becomes aware that the firm may not be compliant with MA requirements (whether or not those requirements are within the scope of the auditor’s opinion o

If a firm materially changes its approach to calculating the MA, then the PRA would usually expect this to be discussed by the firm with the supervisory team.13 However, consistent with Legacy SS7/13,
The expectations for auditor communication in paragraphs 4.2J and [4.2K]
